meta.title: 7.3 Batch Mode — Fan Out
meta.description: Apply the same instruction across many files or targets simultaneously with batch operations.

blocks.prompt: You need to make the same type of change to 40 files — update a header format, add a disclaimer paragraph, or standardize a naming convention. How would you approach this today? What could go wrong if you do them one at a time?

blocks.content:
## The mass-update problem

Some tasks are not complex — they are **repetitive**. You need the same change applied consistently across dozens or hundreds of targets. Doing them one at a time is tedious and error-prone. Doing them manually invites inconsistency.

Batch mode solves this: give Claude one instruction and a set of targets, and it fans the work out across all of them.

## How batch mode works

The `/batch` command takes an instruction and applies it to multiple targets. The basic pattern:

```
/batch "Add a copyright header with the current year to every file" across all .md files in docs/
```

Claude identifies the target files, applies the instruction to each one, and reports the results. The instruction is the same for every target — what changes is the target file.

## When batch mode is the right tool

**Consistent formatting**: Apply the same style changes across all documents in a folder. Every file gets the same treatment.

**Mass updates**: A company name changed, a date format needs updating, a disclaimer needs adding. One instruction, dozens of files.

**Large-scale refactoring**: Rename a concept across an entire project. Update every reference to the old name in every file.

**Template application**: You have a new template and need existing files to conform. Batch mode restructures each file to match.

**Standardization**: Different team members used different conventions. Batch mode brings everything into alignment.

## The instruction matters enormously

Batch mode amplifies your instruction — for better or worse. A vague instruction applied 40 times creates 40 vaguely modified files. A precise instruction applied 40 times creates 40 perfectly consistent files.

Good batch instructions:
- Specify exactly what to change and what to preserve
- Include the expected format or template
- Define edge cases ("If the file already has a header, replace it rather than adding a second one")
- State what success looks like

**Weak**: "Fix the formatting in these files"

**Strong**: "In each file, ensure the title is an H1 heading, add a blank line after the title, convert any bullet lists to use dashes instead of asterisks, and ensure the file ends with a single newline. Do not change any content — only formatting."

## Batch mode vs. find-and-replace

You might wonder: why not just use find-and-replace? The answer is that batch mode understands **context**.

Find-and-replace is literal: it changes text pattern X to text pattern Y. Batch mode understands what it is reading. It can:

- Add a paragraph that makes sense in context, not just paste the same text
- Restructure differently depending on the file's current state
- Handle edge cases where files are structured differently
- Make intelligent decisions about files that do not fit the pattern

This is the difference between a search-replace tool and an agent that reads, understands, and edits each file individually while maintaining consistency across all of them.

## Practical strategies

### Start with a dry run
Before batch-editing 100 files, test your instruction on 2-3 files manually. Confirm the result is what you want, then batch the rest.

### Review in bulk
After a batch operation, use `git diff` to review all changes at once. Look for patterns — if one file was handled differently, investigate why.

### Group similar targets
If your 40 files fall into 3 categories that need slightly different treatment, run 3 batch operations with category-specific instructions rather than one generic one.

### Set expectations for time
Batch operations that touch many files take proportionally more time and tokens. A batch across 50 files is roughly 50x the cost of editing one file. Factor this into your context budget planning.

blocks.question: You run a batch operation to add a disclaimer to 30 markdown files. After it finishes, you check and find that 28 files look perfect, but 2 files have the disclaimer in the wrong location. What went wrong and what should you do?
blocks.answer: The 2 files likely had a **different structure** than the other 28 — perhaps a different heading hierarchy or an unusual layout that confused the placement logic. This is normal with batch operations: edge cases exist. Fix those 2 files individually with a targeted instruction that accounts for their specific structure. For future batches, preview a few files first to identify structural variations and either handle them in your instruction ('if the file starts with frontmatter, place the disclaimer after it') or group them into a separate batch.

blocks.title: Identify a batch-appropriate task in your work: something that needs the same change across multiple files or documents. Write the batch instruction you would use. Then stress-test it: What edge cases could exist? What files might be structured differently? Revise your instruction to handle those cases.
blocks.solution: Strong batch instructions anticipate variation. If you wrote 'add a header to every file,' revise it to 'add a header to every file — if a header already exists, replace it; if the file starts with YAML frontmatter, place the header after the closing ---; if the file is empty, skip it.' The more edge cases your instruction handles, the more consistent your batch results will be. Testing on 2-3 representative files before the full batch is always worth the time.

questions: What repetitive task in your work have you been putting off because it involves too many files?
How does the quality of your batch instruction compare to the quality of a one-off instruction? Why does precision matter more for batch operations?
What is the trade-off between batch speed and manual review? When would you batch-and-review versus do each file individually?

blocks.content: Batch mode fans out one instruction across many targets — consistent, context-aware mass updates that go beyond find-and-replace. The instruction quality matters exponentially: a precise instruction across 40 files gives 40 perfect results; a vague one gives 40 problems. Always dry-run on a few files first and review with git diff after.

blocks.prompt: Batch mode runs inside an interactive session. But what if you want Claude to run without any interaction at all — as part of a script, a cron job, or an automated pipeline? That is non-interactive mode, and it unlocks a completely different category of use.
